nodes.name: Overall Photosynthesis Equation
nodes.truth: Photosynthesis converts light energy into chemical energy, primarily sugar, by using carbon dioxide and water and releasing oxygen as a byproduct, summarized as 6CO2 + 6H2O + Light Energy "," C6H12O6 + 6O2.

nodes.name: Chloroplast Structure
nodes.truth: Chloroplasts are organelles in plant cells characterized by an outer and inner membrane, an intermembrane space, stroma (fluid-filled interior), and thylakoids (sacs) stacked into grana, where photosynthesis occurs.

nodes.name: Light-Dependent Reactions Overview
nodes.truth: The light-dependent reactions occur in the thylakoid membranes, capturing light energy to produce ATP and NADPH, and releasing oxygen from water splitting.

nodes.name: Photosystem II (PSII) Function
nodes.truth: Photosystem II absorbs light, excites electrons, and then splits water molecules (photolysis) to replace these electrons, releasing O2 and H+ ions into the thylakoid lumen.

nodes.name: Thylakoid Electron Transport Chain
nodes.truth: The electron transport chain moves excited electrons from PSII through a series of protein complexes, pumping protons into the thylakoid lumen and creating a proton gradient.

nodes.name: Photosystem I (PSI) Function
nodes.truth: Photosystem I receives electrons from the thylakoid ETC, re-excites them with light energy, and passes them to an enzyme that reduces NADP+ to NADPH.

nodes.name: ATP Synthase and Chemiosmosis
nodes.truth: ATP synthase uses the electrochemical proton gradient generated by the ETC (chemiosmosis) across the thylakoid membrane to drive the synthesis of ATP from ADP and Pi in the stroma.

nodes.name: NADPH Production
nodes.truth: Ferredoxin-NADP+ reductase (FNR) uses the high-energy electrons from Photosystem I, along with H+ ions, to reduce NADP+ to NADPH in the stroma.

nodes.name: Calvin Cycle Overview
nodes.truth: The Calvin cycle occurs in the stroma, using the ATP and NADPH produced during the light reactions to fix atmospheric carbon dioxide into glucose precursors through a series of enzymatic reactions.

nodes.name: Carbon Fixation (Rubisco)
nodes.truth: In the first step of the Calvin cycle, the enzyme RuBisCO catalyzes the attachment of CO2 to an existing five-carbon sugar, RuBP, forming an unstable six-carbon intermediate that immediately splits into two molecules of 3-PGA.

nodes.name: Reduction Phase of Calvin Cycle
nodes.truth: The 3-PGA molecules from carbon fixation are phosphorylated by ATP and then reduced by NADPH to form G3P, a three-carbon sugar, with some G3P exiting the cycle to synthesize glucose.

nodes.name: Regeneration Phase of Calvin Cycle
nodes.truth: The remaining G3P molecules are rearranged through a series of enzyme-catalyzed reactions, consuming ATP, to regenerate RuBP, allowing the Calvin cycle to continue.

nodes.name: Photorespiration
nodes.truth: Photorespiration is a wasteful process where RuBisCO binds O2 instead of CO2, leading to the consumption of ATP and the release of CO2 without producing sugar.

nodes.name: C4 Photosynthesis Adaptation
nodes.truth: C4 plants minimize photorespiration by spatially separating carbon fixation (in mesophyll cells using PEP carboxylase to form a 4-carbon compound) from the Calvin cycle (in bundle-sheath cells).

nodes.name: CAM Photosynthesis Adaptation
nodes.truth: CAM plants minimize water loss and photorespiration by temporally separating carbon fixation (at night, storing malate) from the Calvin cycle (during the day, releasing CO2 from malate in the same cell).
